does: This paper examines governance frameworks and best practices for managing agentic AI systems—autonomous agents that can act with limited human oversight. It synthesizes existing regulatory approaches, institutional mechanisms, and technical safeguards used to govern AI systems that operate independently across domains like robotics, autonomous vehicles, and decision-making systems. The work identifies key governance patterns including pre-deployment review, runtime monitoring, liability structures, and stakeholder involvement. It presents case studies and comparative analysis of how different organizations and jurisdictions approach oversight of these increasingly autonomous systems, highlighting tensions between enabling innovation and ensuring safety, as well as gaps in current governance structures.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research in AI governance, safety, and ethics while extending it specifically to agentic systems. It connects to prior work on AI regulation frameworks like the EU AI Act and NIST AI Risk Management Framework, as well as decades of governance literature for other complex autonomous technologies—particularly aerospace, nuclear energy, and autonomous vehicles. It also draws from technical AI safety research on interpretability, robustness, and containment, recognizing that governance must be paired with technical capabilities. The paper responds to the growing recognition that traditional governance approaches designed for human decision-making or static software may be insufficient for systems that adapt and act autonomously.
enables: This framework enables more systematic and evidence-based design of governance structures as agentic AI becomes more prevalent. It provides a foundation for policymakers, organizations, and technologists to anticipate governance challenges and design appropriate oversight mechanisms before widespread deployment. The work could guide development of international standards for agentic AI governance, inform corporate governance best practices, and help align technical safety research with institutional oversight needs. It also opens pathways for future research into adaptive governance models that can evolve as agent capabilities change, cross-domain governance principles, and mechanisms for ensuring accountability when agents make consequential decisions.
